home.aboutCopy:
I recently graduated from Duke University's M.A. in Digital Art History / Computational Media.

My work begins with research and evolves into interactive experiences. Whether reconstructing ancient architecture, designing immersive environments, or building digital prototypes, I enjoy transforming complex ideas into experiences that people can explore, understand, and remember.

Working across disciplines has become one of my greatest strengths. I move comfortably between historical research, visual design, 3D production, and technical implementation, using AI as a creative collaborator while keeping thoughtful design and human experience at the center of every project.
home.heroCopy: I create digital experiences by combining design, technology, and storytelling, drawing inspiration from history, culture, and the ways people connect with them.
home.heroImageAlt: A flock of birds flying above water and a distant shoreline
home.aboutImageAlt: Loren wearing a virtual reality headset
home.marcellusImageAlt: XR reconstruction of the Theater of Marcellus

photography.heading: Photography.
photography.intro: Observations from research, making, and life in motion.

bridges.heading: Parallel Bridges: A Cultural Dialogue
bridges.paragraphOne: Inspired by the Asiatic Arboretum at Duke Gardens, which predominantly features Japanese influences, this project aims to create a more balanced representation of Asian culture by incorporating authentic Chinese architectural elements. The addition of a zigzag stone bridge and a polygonal pavilion introduces a contrasting yet complementary vision of Chinese architecture, often underrepresented in the space.
bridges.paragraphTwo: The design incorporates a Chinese-style bridge placed parallel to the iconic red wooden bridge, offering a meaningful juxtaposition that highlights the unique cultural symbolism of each structure. Created using Maya and presented through an augmented reality (AR) experience via Adobe Aero, the installation allows users to interact with the space in real time. As users reach a designated spot and place their anchor, the stairs, stone bridge, and pavilion gradually appear on their phone screens, simulating the process of integrating architectural elements into the natural landscape.
bridges.paragraphThree: This sequential reveal not only emphasizes the presence of Chinese heritage but also invites reflection on how cultural symbols shape our perception of place. By contrasting the Chinese bridge with the Japanese red bridge, the project fosters deeper contemplation about cultural representation and the visual dynamics of mixed heritage.
bridges.showcaseHeading: A bridge appears in the landscape.
bridges.showcaseCopy: The digital pavilion and bridge are anchored into the garden so their appearance unfolds from the visitor's physical point of view.
bridges.accessCopy: Use a supported device to scan the Adobe Aero code and view Parallel Bridges in the garden.
bridges.qrCaption: Scan to open in Adobe Aero.
bridges.heroImageAlt: A phone presenting the Parallel Bridges augmented-reality experience at Duke Gardens

marcellus.metaTitle: Research → Design → Build: the Theater of Marcellus and XR — Loren Zheng
marcellus.walkthroughCaption: A walkthrough of the project, screen-recorded through Meta Quest 3, showcasing the immersive AR/VR reconstruction of the Theater of Marcellus.
marcellus.heading: Research → Design → Build: the Theater of Marcellus and XR
marcellus.lede: This project leverages immersive digital technologies, virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR), to reimagine the Theater of Marcellus, one of ancient Rome's most significant monuments.
marcellus.paragraphOne: This project leverages immersive digital technologies, virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR), to reimagine the Theater of Marcellus, one of ancient Rome's most significant monuments. By integrating digital reconstruction and interactive experiences, the project explores how Roman spatiality, architectural hierarchy, and political messaging shaped the theater's function and audience experience.
marcellus.paragraphTwo: Using Blender for 3D modeling and Unity for development, the experience is implemented on Meta Quest 3, allowing users to navigate the theater's structure and engage as Roman spectators. The project highlights the strictly structured seating arrangements, orchestrated movement through space, and the theater's role in imperial display, demonstrating how architecture reinforced social stratification.
marcellus.paragraphThree: Beyond visualization, this project examines the balance between scholarly accuracy and interactive engagement, offering a deeper understanding of the theater's historical and cultural significance. Future expansions could introduce narrative-driven experiences and broader accessibility efforts, ensuring that digital reconstructions serve both academic inquiry and public engagement in meaningful ways.
marcellus.contextCaption: By referencing academic illustration and maps, I positioned monuments within my scene to reflect their relative historical locations. This is also the starting point for users in the immersive environment, where they can experience a similar vantage point to see significant monuments in context and their spatial relationships.
marcellus.modelingCaption: Modeling process, referencing other scholars' reconstruction.
marcellus.overlayCaption: Overlay mode for users to inspect the model with different levels of reconstruction certainty.
marcellus.arrivalCaption: When users are transported into the immersive scene, they are at the northwest side of the theater; historical information and a map are provided through the interface.
marcellus.roleAccessCaption: Users are restricted from teleporting to areas that do not align with their assigned role.
marcellus.timeCaption: Freedom to change the time of the day.
marcellus.wildBeastCaption: Wild beast hunt performance with pivoting opening scene building.
marcellus.freedmenCaption: When users choose to play the “Freedmen” role.

reassembled.heading: reAssembled
reassembled.subtitle: A Virtual Exhibition of Reunited Antiquities
reassembled.paragraphOne: Using Artsteps, this is a virtual exhibition that explores the fragmentation, displacement, and potential reunification of selected antiquities from the ancient Mediterranean and ancient West Asia.
reassembled.paragraphTwo: By bringing together scattered artifacts, such as the Parthenon Marbles and the Ishtar Gate reliefs, the exhibition invites visitors to consider the impact of colonial-era collecting, museum practices, and ongoing repatriation debates. Through putting together 3D models, archival images, and contextual text, the project aims to provide a new perspective on these objects and their histories.
reassembled.paragraphThree: While this exhibition is a digital approximation rather than a definitive reconstruction, it hopes to encourage discussion about the ways antiquities are collected, displayed, and remembered. reAssembled does not claim to resolve complex debates but rather seeks to offer a thoughtful space for visitors to engage with the challenges of cultural heritage and imagine possibilities for reconnection.

rhythms.metaTitle: Rhythms of Life: Human & Plant Connection — Loren Zheng
rhythms.heading: Rhythms of Life: Human & Plant Connection
rhythms.subtitle: Circuit Playground microcontroller
rhythms.paragraphOne: This project explores the interplay between human and plant life through real-time biometric interaction. Using a Circuit Playground microcontroller, I captured live pulse data via light-based photoplethysmography and visualized it in TouchDesigner. The heartbeat animates a pulsating digital heart while simultaneously influencing a dynamic flower, whose petals expand and reset with each pulse, mirroring natural growth cycles.
rhythms.paragraphTwo: By syncing biological and botanical rhythms, the piece highlights the interconnectedness of living systems despite their differing forms of mobility. Through this real-time interaction, technology becomes a bridge, making visible the unseen relationships between human and environmental vitality. The project serves as both an artistic meditation and a speculative reflection on our shared dependency on ecological balance.
rhythms.circuitCaption: Circuit Playground used to capture live pulse data.
rhythms.touchdesignerAlt: TouchDesigner visualization of a pulse-responsive flower beside a Circuit Playground microcontroller

writing.heading: Theses
writing.intro: A collection of research exploring art history, gender, cultural heritage, and digital media.
writing.marcellusTitle: Affordances of Extended Reality in Cultural Heritage: Rethinking the Theater of Marcellus Through AR and VR
writing.marcellusSummary: Master’s thesis examining how immersive technologies can create new forms of engagement with historic sites and their layered histories.
writing.mirrorTitle: The Mirror: A Feminist Reading of Rubens
writing.mirrorSummary: A research paper exploring mirrors, gender, and the gaze in Peter Paul Rubens’ depictions of women.
writing.mirrorFull: This paper explores Peter Paul Rubens’ use of mirrors and the gaze in his depictions of women, particularly in Venus at a Mirror. While mirrors in art have long been associated with vanity and the idealization of female beauty, Rubens’ approach complicates this tradition. By applying theories of the gaze from Foucault, Lacan, and Mulvey, this study examines how Rubens’ work engages with gender roles and power dynamics. While Rubens is often known for his sensualized portrayals of women, this paper investigates whether his use of mirrors subverts or reinforces dominant cultural norms. Through a close analysis of iconography, biography, and artistic context, this research sheds new light on Rubens’ representation of women and the function of mirrors in his visual narrative.
